title: practical english usage - Part 54
content:
structures.

When  we talk about the past, we  generally use the past perfect in these I'm flying to New York tomorrow. It'll be the first time I've travelled by plane.

It's one of the most interesting books I've ever read.

That's the third cake you've eaten this morning.

This is the fifth time you've asked me the same question (     This-is the-fifth volcano:) not This is the first time I've seen a volcano, (     This is my first time^to see a heanher-sing:) not This is the first time that I've heard her sing, (     This is the first time that I second/third/only/best/worst, etc.

We   use the present perfect in sentences constructed with this/it/that is the first/ 1  This     is  the    first     time,     etc 56 This     is  the    first/last       .. ., etc knowing-hen...) not / hadn't known her for very long when we got married, (     /  hadn't been meaning is one for which  a progressive form would be more suitable.

Some verbs are not normally used in progressive forms (►  4), even if the I had read all my magazines, and was beginning to get bored.

I had been reading science fiction, and my mind was full of strange images.

simple tenses to emphasise the idea of completion. Compare: Progressive forms generally emphasise the continuation of an activity; we  use The tree that blew down had stood therefor 300 years.

My legs were stiff because I had been standing still for a long time.

simple forms. Compare: situations. When  we  talk about longer-lasting or permanent situations we  prefer Progressive forms are mostly used to talk about more temporary actions and 4 progressive         and   simple:    differences Past   and   Perfect     Tenses     Section 5 grammar •  Section 6  Passives reflexive verbs: e.g. I was bom in 1956; English is spoken here.

the door opened. And some English passives might be translated by active or certain other languages: e.g. My shoes are wearing out; She is sitting; Suddenly Some English active verbs might be translated by passive or reflexive verbs in suffers, you are really saying that something is done to him/her.

'active* meanings; for instance, if you say that somebody receives something or Meaning and grammar do not always go together. Not all active verbs have meaning       and   grammar This house was built in 1486 by Sir John Latton.

happens in an expression with by; the noun is called the 'agent' (►  58).

corresponding passive sentence. If it does have to be expressed, this usually In most cases, the subject of an active verb is not expressed in the SUBJECT Passive: This house was built in I486.

Active:   They built this house in 1486.

The  object of an active verb corresponds to the subject of a passive verb.

repaired, will be changed.

to be the subject (►  67), we  use a passive verb: was built, is spoken, is being speak, is repairing, will change. If we  want B (the 'receiver' of the action) want A  (the 'doer' or 'agent') to be the subject, we  use an active verb: built, When  A  does something to B, there are often two ways to talk about it. If we Your life will be changed by this book. (passive) - This book will change your life. (active) The roof is being repaired by a friend of ours. (passive) - A friend of ours is repairing the roof. (active) German is spoken by most Austrians. (passive) - Most Austrians speak German. (active) This house was built in 1486. (passive) - They built this house in 1486.
